column3: Zeus battles Typhon, restoring cosmic order
column6: Paralleled in Norse (Thor vs. Jörmungandr), Vedic (Indra vs. Vritra), Judeo-Christian (Yahweh vs. Leviathan), Egyptian (Ra vs. Apep), Chinese (Yu vs. Xiangliu)1.

column3: Thor battles the world serpent Jörmungandr
Indra slays serpent Vritra to release waters
Yahweh defeats Leviathan, symbolizing victory over chaos
Ra battles chaos serpent Apep nightly
Yu the Great defeats the nine-headed serpent Xiangliu

column3: Serpent/dragon eating its own tail, symbolizing eternity and cycles

Treasure hidden in a field, found and valued above all

Story of rich man stealing poor man’s lamb to confront King David
Two eagles and a vine, symbolizing Israel and its fate

Comparative Motifs: Many myths and parables share themes across Eurasia, such as the chaoskampf (hero vs. serpent/dragon), death and resurrection, and the use of parables for moral or spiritual teaching123.
